data.title: Royal Bank Lays Off Employees, Promotes Others With Changes to Organization
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

Royal Bank of Canada has laid off employees and made organizational changes in recent months to better position itself for growth.

The bank, Canada's largest by market value and one of the biggest in North America, said that since making executive appointments and platform changes last summer it has continued to make moves to take advantage of Royal Bank's global scale and simplify how it operates

"We have promoted a number of colleagues, expanded responsibilities and made stronger connections across our platforms. With these changes, some difficult decisions have been made and as a result some colleagues were impacted and left the bank," a bank spokesman said. He added the lender has provided support and assistance to those affected.

The number of employees laid off wasn't disclosed.

The spokesman said the changes aren't related to last year's roughly $10 billion acquisition of the Canadian assets of HSBC.

Reuters earlier Friday reported the lay offs at Royal Bank, some of which it said were from the lender's technology and operations teams.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Vail Resorts Faces Labor Cost Headwinds Despite Favorable Weather Conditions, UBS Says
data.description:
Vail Resorts is unlikely to be able to meet the upper end of its full-year EBITDA guidance due to labor cost headwinds, UBS said in a note Friday.

Investors will be on the look out Monday whether the company reiterates its EBITDA guidance when it reports its quarterly results. Last year, the company reiterated its EBITDA guidance in January, but guided down when Q2 was reported, given challenging weather conditions, UBS said.

The company saw favorable weather to start 2025, with snow cover acting as catalyst to more visitations to its ski resorts, UBS said.

The firm said it expects labor cost headwinds to impact the company's EBITDA by at least $20 million to $25 million during the year, making the upper end of the company's guidance of $838 million to $894 million "not as achievable."

Still, the firm said its EBITDA estimate of $870 million was within the guidance range and slightly below Street estimates of $871 million.

The firm has a neutral rating on the stock with a price target of $185.

data.title: US Credit Growth Surpasses Expectations in January
data.description:
Total US consumer credit rose by $18.08 billion in January 2025, following a downwardly revised increase of $37.05 billion in December 2024, and exceeding market expectations of $14.5 billion.

This resulted in a seasonally adjusted annual growth rate of 4.3 percent for consumer credit.

Revolving credit experienced a robust annual increase of 8.2 percent, while nonrevolving credit grew at a more modest annual rate of 3 percent.

The data suggests strong consumer borrowing activity, particularly in revolving credit, reflecting continued confidence in short-term spending despite fluctuations in credit growth from the previous month.

data.title: Consumers borrow a lot less in January
data.description:
Total consumer credit rose at a 4.3% annual rate in January, down from 8.3% gain in the prior month, the Federal Reserve said Friday.

Credit-card borrowing slowed to an 8.2% rate from 18.5% rate in December.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates.

DJIA SPX

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Appian Gets Virginia Supreme Court Hearing on Reinstating $2.04 Billion Verdict Against Pegasystems
data.description:
Appian said Friday that the Supreme Court of Virginia agreed to hear its petition to reinstate a jury's $2.04 billion judgment against Pegasystems for trade secret misappropriation.

The court agreed to review four issues raised in the Court of Appeals decision.

Appian sued Pegasystems to protect its intellectual property. A jury found that Pegasystems used a "spy" to get trade secrets, benefiting financially from the misappropriation, Appian said.

The Supreme Court also agreed to review Pegasystems' cross-appeal on trade secret misappropriation, though lower courts rejected its arguments, Appian said.

Pegasystems didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

Shares of Appian rose 3.1% in recent Friday trading, and Pegasystems fell 0.3%.

data.title: How Weak Growth Forecasts Can Immediately Hurt Bank Earnings: Heard on the Street — WSJ
data.description:
By Jonathan Weil

It's been a tough several days for bank stocks. The pain could start showing up in banks' credit losses and earnings soon if one of the Federal Reserve's closely watched economic indicators is on the mark.

The KBW Bank Index is down around 9% this week. At the same time, the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta's running estimate for first-quarter gross domestic product, called GDPNow, is negative 2.4%. As recently as Feb. 19, the GDPNow model was estimating 2.3% growth. Two consecutive quarterly contractions would meet a commonly accepted definition of a recession. The U.S is a long way from that at the moment, but clearly the direction of economic forecasts is trending sharply downward.

Swiftly changing GDP forecasts could have a significant impact on lenders' credit loss estimates long before borrower defaults start rising in great numbers. Five years ago, new U.S. accounting standards took effect that were designed to make lenders recognize credit losses more quickly. From day one, they are supposed to continuously estimate their credit losses over the life of a given instrument, be it a loan or bond.

At large banks, that often means using mathematical models that directly incorporate forecasts of economic growth as a key input. For lenders that don't have large staffs of economists on the payroll, it has become common to outsource this part of their models to companies such as Moody's Analytics, which provide off-the-shelf tools to help clients estimate their expected credit losses.

Some banks provide granular details in their earnings presentations quantifying the impact that changes in economic forecasts had on the size of their credit-loss allowances for any given period.

For example, BankUnited, based in Miami Lakes, Fla., last year increased its credit-loss allowance to $223.2 million as of Dec. 31 from $202.7 million at the end of 2023. A bar chart in its yearend earnings presentation showed six different drivers of the change, both positive and negative. One of the positive drivers was management's economic forecast, which improved during the year and reduced the size of BankUnited's allowance by $15.8 million. That helped to offset other factors that caused the allowance to go up, such as growing losses on specific loans.

Not all banks provide the same level of disclosure about how their economic forecasts impact their credit-loss estimates and earnings. But they will be going through similar exercises when the first quarter closes. Soon they may need to start sharply revising the economic forecasts they use in their credit-loss estimates. This week's drop in banks' stock prices reflects some of that.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Wall St edges higher after Fed chief's comments, but set for weekly loss
data.description:
Key points:
  • Hewlett Packard slumps after dour second-quarter forecasts
  • Nonfarm payrolls increase by 151,000 in February
  • Indexes up: Dow +0.46%, S&P 500 +0.43%, Nasdaq +0.54%

U.S. stocks edged higher on Friday, rebounding from early declines after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the economy was "in a good place."

But stocks were headed for a weekly loss as Powell said the central bank will not be quick to cut interest rates and echoed investor concerns about President Donald Trump's policies. Markets have been roiled this week by uncertainty about Trump's trade policy, particularly tariff decisions on imported goods from Canada, Mexico and China. Powell said the Fed will take a cautious approach to monetary policy easing, adding that the economy currently "continues to be in a good place". "Powell is echoing what the rest of us feel: unease that while the adjustments made by the administration may well work and put the country on better financial footing, the speed and whipsaw-like nature of the change makes it difficult to predict and to plan around," said Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group in Richmond, Virginia. "So, the best action when that occurs is to sit and wait." Stocks fell in choppy early trade, but rebounded after Powell's comments. Among the benchmark S&P 500 11 main sectors, utilities , energy , technology and industrials were advancing. Consumer discretionary , financials and consumer staples were dropping. The three main indexes were set to end the week lower, with the Nasdaq on track to finish nearly 4% down, which would be its biggest weekly drop since September. In the previous session, the Nasdaq confirmed a 10% drop from its December all-time high. At 2:55 p.m., the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 197.08 points, or 0.46%, to 42,776.16, the S&P 500 gained 24.86 points, or 0.43%, to 5,763.38 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 99.41 points, or 0.54%, to 18,167.37. Data early on Friday showed U.S. job growth picked up in February from the previous month. However, thousands of recent firings of federal workers were not reflected in the data.

Unemployment ticked up to 4.1%, adding to worries about the economy's resilience. Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have lowered their growth forecasts for the economy. "This is a growth scare," said Adam Hetts, portfolio manager at Janus Henderson Investors. "This is what it feels like to go from a no-landing to a soft-landing environment and it's unpleasant. It involves a spate of unpleasant economic data, and the primary driver being weaker consumer spending." On Thursday, Trump offered a four-week reprieve on tariffs he imposed on imports from Canada and Mexico that fall under a free-trade pact. The U.S. remains in a trade war with China.

Reciprocal trade barriers and other duties are expected to take effect in the following weeks. Hewlett Packard Enterprise slumped 13% after saying its annual profit forecast would be hit by U.S. tariffs. Costco fell 7% after the retailer missed Street estimates on quarterly earnings as merchandise costs increased. Broadcom gained 7.6% after the chipmaker assuaged investor worries about artificial intelligence infrastructure demand with a strong second-quarter forecast.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.32-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 75 new highs and 121 new lows on the NYSE.

The S&P 500 posted 8 new 52-week highs and 13 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 21 new highs and 148 new lows.

data.title: Canada Expects Nearly All U.S. Exports to Be USMCA Compliant
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

Canadian officials believe nearly all the country's goods and services that enter the U.S. will do so tariff-free during the latest White House reprieve until April 2.

The White House said Thursday that it would delay implementation of a 25% tariff on imports of Canadian nonenergy goods, and a 10% levy on energy products, until early next month.

However, U.S. officials said the reprieve would only apply to goods that comply with the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade pact, or USMCA, adding that 62% of Canadian imports are ineligible. Hence, those goods face a 25% tariff.

Canadian officials say the discrepancy can be explained by so-called red tape, with some exporters opting not to fill out the appropriate paperwork. Exporters have a choice of sending goods to the U.S. either under the rules set out in USMCA or other trade pacts, or through the World Trade Organization's most-favored nation process. Companies can no longer use the WTO process under the White House order.

"The vast majority of Canadian exports to the United States are, or can quickly be, USMCA compliant," Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc told Canadian Broadcasting Corp. Canadian exporters that used the most-favored nation route had decided that paying a 2.5% duty was worth the cost of not filing the necessary documents, he said.

"Now that there is a difference, I suspect everybody's calling their lawyers get very quickly into compliance," said Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: 14 Books We Read This Week
data.description:
By WSJ Books Staff

Mellon vs. Churchill: The Payback Problem

At the end of World War I, Britain was in heavy financial debt to the U.S. The question of repayments would bedevil both countries for decades. Review by Benn Steil

Read the review

A Fire in His Soul: Van Gogh's Paris Transformation

Miles Unger follows Vincent van Gogh's time living in the bohemian Montmartre district, and its explosive effect on the painter's work. Review by Dan Hofstadter

Read the review

'Hard Neighbors': Young America's Scotch-Irish Border Clans

In the woodland communities that once marked the frontier of the United States, settlers from Scotland and Northern Ireland set out to make an uneasy living. Review by Peter Cozzens

Read the review

'Waiting on the Moon': Peter Wolf on the Prowl

The J. Geils Band singer's memoir recounts his years performing with musical royalty and partying with the world. Review by Dave Shiflett

Read the review

Fiction: 'In a Yellow Wood' by Cynthia Ozick

The writer's hefty collection of short stories and essays defies boundaries and offers a bold retrospective on her own long literary career. Review by Sam Sacks

Read the review

'Expect Great Things!': That Katherine Gibbs Touch

The products of the 20th century's most well-known secretarial school were prized not only for typing skills. Gibbs graduates carried the aura of the elite. Review by Joanne Kaufman

Read the review

'Isle of Ever': An Inheritance, a Puzzle and Pirates Too

In Jen Calonita's novel for young readers, a teenage girl discovers that she's heir to a fortune — if she can solve the riddles left behind by her ancestor. Review by Katie Daniels

Read the review

'The Café With No Name': A Refuge in Vienna

In Robert Seethaler's novel, one man's ambition is to create a place he and others can call home. Review by Heller McAlpin

Read the review

'Righteous Strife': Calling Heaven to the Union's Cause

Facing the crisis of the Civil War, Abraham Lincoln drew on religious feeling to help galvanize the North — and to bolster his own resolve. Review by Amanda Brickell Bellows

Read the review

Mysteries: 'Head Cases' by John McMahon

Plus John Shannon's "Boystown" and Allison Epstein's "Fagin the Thief." Review by Tom Nolan

Read the review

'Lion': The Prodigal Father

In Sonya Walger's work of autobiographical fiction, a daughter's affection for her mercurial father proves a constant in the midst of a turbulent life. Review by Anna Mundow

Read the review

'Under the Same Stars': Tree of Mystery

In Libba Bray's novel for young adults, the disappearance of a pair of teens in 1940s Germany leaves traces that are picked up over the years to come. Review by Katie Daniels

Read the review

Five Best: On Women in Science

Selected by Olivia Campbell, the author, most recently, of "Sisters in Science."

Read the article

Get great reading recommendations delivered to your inbox. Sign up for the weekly Books newsletter.

data.title: MUNCY COLUMBIA FINANCIAL Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Muncy Columbia Financial Corporation, headquartered in Bloomsburg, Pennsylvania, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing a year of significant financial growth and strategic initiatives. The company, which operates as a financial holding company with its primary subsidiary, Journey Bank, provides a range of financial services including deposit accounts, consumer, commercial, and mortgage loans, and operates a full-service trust department. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance, strategic merger activities, and the challenges it faces in the current economic environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest and Dividend Income: $81.7 million, reflecting a significant increase from the previous year due to higher earning asset volumes and yields.
  • Net Interest Income: $50.5 million, an increase of 116.9% from the prior year, driven by higher interest income and controlled interest expenses.
  • Net Income: $19.0 million, a substantial increase compared to $3.4 million in 2023, primarily due to increased net interest income and reduced merger-related expenses.
  • Earnings Per Share - Basic and Diluted: $5.33, up from $1.49 in 2023, reflecting improved profitability and operational efficiency.
  • Total Non-Interest Income: $10.4 million, up 45.7% from the previous year, driven by increased service charges, interchange fees, and earnings on bank-owned life insurance.
  • Total Non-Interest Expense: $37.7 million, an increase of 56.2% from 2023, largely due to the impact of the MBF merger and associated costs.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: Muncy Columbia Financial Corporation operates as a financial holding company with its primary subsidiary, Journey Bank, offering a range of services including deposit accounts, consumer, commercial, and mortgage loans, and a full-service trust department.
  • Merger Activity: In 2023, the company was formed through a strategic merger of equals between CCFNB Bancorp, Inc. and Muncy Bank Financial, Inc., resulting in the formation of Journey Bank, which now operates 22 branch offices across several counties in Pennsylvania.
  • Branch Network: Journey Bank operates 22 branch offices located in the Pennsylvania counties of Clinton, Columbia, Lycoming, Montour, and Northumberland, serving individuals and small to medium-sized businesses.
  • Regulatory Environment: The Corporation and the Bank are subject to a variety of federal and state regulations, including the Gramm Leach Bliley Act, the Bank Holding Company Act, and the Dodd-Frank Act, among others.
  • Human Capital: As of December 31, 2024, the Corporation employed 236 full-time equivalent employees, emphasizing equal opportunity employment and maintaining good employee relations.
  • Operational Focus: The Corporation's business philosophy centers on providing personal attention and professional financial assistance, with a focus on local decision-making and flexible credit policies.
  • Future Outlook: The Corporation anticipates continued growth in its loan portfolio and expects to maintain capital levels that exceed regulatory standards for well-capitalized institutions. The Bank plans to continue migrating customer repurchase accounts into interest-bearing deposits in 2025.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Merger with Muncy Bank Financial, Inc.: The company completed a merger with Muncy Bank Financial, Inc., significantly expanding its operations and market presence in Northcentral Pennsylvania. This strategic move included the acquisition of assets totaling $671.4 million and liabilities of $634.0 million, enhancing the company's scale and competitive position.
  • Capital Management: The company increased its total stockholders' equity by $12.6 million, primarily through earnings retention and a decrease in accumulated other comprehensive loss. It also repurchased $1.5 million of its own shares and declared and paid dividends of $1.76 per share in 2024, up from $1.71 per share in 2023.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to maintain capital levels that exceed regulatory standards for well-capitalized institutions, with a focus on sustaining a strong financial condition. It anticipates continued migration of customer repurchase accounts into interest-bearing deposits, improving liquidity. The company also intends to explore further strategic opportunities for growth and capital investment, while managing interest rate risk and maintaining a balanced asset/liability mix.

Challenges and Risks

  • Industry Challenges: The financial services industry is facing significant challenges due to recent failures of several institutions in 2023, leading to decreased confidence among depositors and investors. This has resulted in increased volatility in equity and other securities markets, potentially impacting the Corporation's liquidity, net interest margins, and credit losses.
  • Economic Conditions: Economic conditions, particularly in central Pennsylvania, pose a risk to the Corporation's business. An economic downturn could lead to increased loan delinquencies, reduced demand for products and services, and a decline in collateral values, adversely affecting the Corporation's financial condition.
  • Competition: The Corporation faces intense competition from larger financial institutions with greater resources, which could impact its ability to retain and grow its client base. This competition may lead to reduced net income due to decreased loan originations and increased interest rates on deposits.
  • Liquidity Needs: The Corporation relies heavily on customer deposits and loan repayments. Changes in economic conditions or interest rates could affect deposit levels, necessitating reliance on secondary sources of liquidity, which may not always be adequate.
  • Interest Rate Fluctuations: Interest rate fluctuations pose a significant risk to the Corporation's profitability. A significant change in interest rates could adversely affect net interest income, and the Corporation may not be able to effectively manage these changes.
  • Credit Losses: The Corporation's allowance for credit losses may not be adequate, as actual credit losses could exceed estimates, resulting in a material adverse effect on the Corporation's financial condition and results of operations.
  • Regulatory Risks: Compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act and other anti-money laundering statutes is critical. Deficiencies in compliance could lead to fines, restrictions on business operations, and reputational damage.
  • Technological and Cybersecurity Threats: Technological advances and cybersecurity threats are ongoing concerns. Any failure or breach in the Corporation's information systems could result in significant financial and reputational damage.
  • Merger Integration Risks: The recent merger with Muncy Bank Financial, Inc. presents integration risks, including potential exposure to unknown liabilities and asset quality issues. Management is focused on realizing the expected benefits of the merger, such as cost savings and increased market presence.

SEC Filing: MUNCY COLUMBIA FINANCIAL Corp [ CCFN ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Argentina Industrial Output Surges for 2nd Month
data.description:
Industrial production in Argentina surged 7.1% year-on-year in January 2025, marking a second consecutive month of growth after an 18-month contraction streak, though slowing from December’s 8.4% increase.

Most major sectors remained in expansion but at a moderated pace, including machinery and equipment (10.9% vs. 17.7% in December), manufacturing (25.1% vs. 33.4%), food and beverages (6.6% vs. 8.1%), and equipment, devices, and instruments (50.7% vs. 68.1%), while basic metals (-7.9% vs. -4.9%) was the only sector still contracting.

Meanwhile, oil refining, coke, and nuclear fuel (6.4% vs. 4.1%) and wood, paper, publishing, and printing (1.1% vs. 0.3%) continued to recover.

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, industrial production fell 1.3%, reversing the 0.2% gain from the prior month.
